title: Gauge-Engineered Tunable Mode Selection in Non-Hermitian Directed-Graph Networks
abstract: Non-Hermitian physics enables novel control over open quantum and wave systems, but selectively isolating individual modes without delicate balancing of gain and loss remains challenging. Here we introduce a gauge-engineering method in directed-graph networks that support geometry-protected pure decay modes-eigenstates exhibiting smooth exponential amplitude decay along directed paths. In fully connected configurations, a single dominant mode naturally emerges with a large, tunable energy gap from the rest. By adding synthetic gauge fields via phase-compensated non-reciprocal hopping, we can promote any desired pure decay mode to the dominant position, while preserving its amplitude profile. The approach extends to simultaneous selection of paired modes in half-connected graphs and customizable multi-mode distributions in higher dimensions via orthogonal folding. Our method enables robust, loss/gain-free control over mode profiles, advancing applications in single-mode lasers, sensors, and quantum processing.
summary: This paper presents a gauge-engineering technique to achieve robust, tunable selection of specific eigenmodes in non-Hermitian directed-graph networks. By introducing synthetic gauge fields, the authors can force any desired pure decay mode to become the dominant state, bypassing the need for delicate gain/loss balancing. This advances applications in areas like single-mode lasers and quantum sensing.
main_problem: The core challenge is selectively isolating and controlling individual eigenmodes in open quantum and wave systems, especially in non-Hermitian settings where gain/loss balancing is difficult.
main_result: The authors introduce a gauge-engineering method that robustly promotes any desired pure decay mode to the dominant state, enabling loss/gain-free control over mode profiles.
method: The technique involves adding synthetic gauge fields via phase-compensated non-reciprocal hopping to the directed-graph network Hamiltonian.
model_or_system: The system is modeled using non-Hermitian directed-graph networks. The method is generalized from single-mode selection in fully connected graphs to paired and multi-mode selection in half-connected and higher-dimensional graphs.
key_observables: Pure decay modes (eigenstates with smooth exponential amplitude decay), the energy spectrum, and the ability to tune the dominant mode's energy gap.
important_parameters: The gauge field phase ($	heta_k$), the graph connectivity (fully connected, half-connected), and the hopping parameters ($t$).
main_assumptions: The method relies on the underlying graph geometry supporting pure decay modes, and the gauge field is implemented via phase compensation without altering intrinsic loss/gain.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the emergence of a singular mode in fully connected graphs, demonstrate gauge engineering for single-mode selection, and show extensions to double and multi-mode selection via dimension folding.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the concept of geometry-protected pure decay modes, then detailing the gauge-engineering technique for single-mode selection, and finally generalizing this control to simultaneous paired and multi-mode selection in higher dimensions.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful, generalizable theoretical tool for controlling spectral properties in open quantum systems, directly relevant to designing next-generation single-mode lasers and quantum sensors.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Figure 2(c-d) presents the results of single-mode selection under different gauge configurations. For 𝑘= 1 (𝑘= 3 ), the 2st (4st) mode is clearly selected, with its phase distribution shows 2𝜋 and 6𝜋 winding. Notably, all selected modes retain the same amplitude profile as in the ungauged case (Fig. 1(c)).
